ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic toast class (FOODON_00002473). No specific multigrain kaya toast entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar products. No specific kaya toast or multigrain kaya toast code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for kaya toast or multigrain toast as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving consists of two slices of toasted multigrain bread spread with kaya and butter, weighing approximately 80 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast (HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists kaya toast (white bread) with kaya and butter. Values adapted for multigrain bread variant by substituting multigrain bread nutrient profile (higher fibre, slightly lower GI) from HPB nutrient tables for wholemeal/multigrain bread. Core macros and sodium are anchored to HPB kaya toast data; fibre adjusted upward to reflect multigrain bread. All values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — multigrain bread and kaya toast analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for multigrain kaya toast exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on multigrain bread GI values (range 43–65, central estimate ~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. The addition of kaya (coconut jam, high sugar) and butter (fat) moderately raises the effective GI relative to plain multigrain bread; the estimate of 55 reflects this composite. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (55 × 37.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 20.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~248 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as soft-boiled eggs with soy sauce, as the cumulative sodium from a typical kaya toast breakfast set can approach 600–800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load (~20.5 per serving) driven by refined carbohydrates and added sugar in kaya
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume multigrain kaya toast in controlled portions (one slice), pair with a protein source such as a soft-boiled egg to blunt postprandial glucose response, and account for the approximately 37 g of carbohydrate per 80 g serving in their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~6.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, request reduced butter or substitute with a plant-based spread, and be aware that the saturated fat content (~5.4 g per 80 g serving) represents approximately 27% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain Kaya Toast is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack item consisting of two slices of toasted multigrain bread generously spread with kaya — a traditional Hainanese coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf — and topped with thin slices of cold salted butter. The multigrain bread provides a slightly nutty, hearty flavour and a firmer texture compared to the classic white bread version, with higher dietary fibre (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g) and a marginally lower glycemic response. The dish is a staple of Singaporean kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) culture and is commonly served as part of a breakfast set alongside soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and dark soy sauce, and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). Nutritionally, a standard 80 g serving provides approximately 254 kcal, 37 g of carbohydrate, 9.2 g of fat, and 5.8 g of protein. The kaya spread contributes significant sugar (approximately 12 g per 100 g of the composite), while the butter adds saturated fat. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 248 mg per serving. Clinically, this food is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions but warrants attention for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, moderate GL), hypertension (sodium), or cardiovascular disease (saturated fat). The multigrain variant is preferred over the white bread version for individuals seeking higher fibre intake and a lower glycemic response.

Is kaya toast suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact multigrain kaya toast entry exists in HPB Foodo or any other approved primary source. Values are adapted from HPB kaya toast (white bread) data with multigrain bread nutrient adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database multigrain bread values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, the source anchor (HPB) is a primary approved source, and the multigrain adjustment is well-supported by HPB wholemeal/multigrain bread nutrient tables.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo kaya toast (white bread base) with multigrain bread substitution; not a direct exact match. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this food category. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero — kaya toast contains no meaningful vitamin C source; HPB data confirms negligible/zero vitamin C for this food. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on multigrain bread GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for multigrain kaya toast was found. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 37.2 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 20.5. 7. Halal status is generally applicable for standard kaya toast preparations; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu-style name 'Multigrain Traditional Kaya Toast' to the canonical form 'Multigrain Kaya Toast'. 9. The gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect a glycemic index of 55, which falls within the low GI classification (55 or less).
